content.full_text:
Fern Poltrrcel PRACTICEs CouvtlsSIoN
428 J Street . Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
August 17,2004
Richarci R. Rudnansky
Meyers, Nave, Riback,
Silver & Wilson
401 Mendocino Avenue, Suite 100
Santa Rosa, CA 95401
Re
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-04-160
Dear Mr. Rudnansky:
This letter is in response to your request for informal assistance on behalf of Lois
Fisher, Planning Commissioner for the Town of Windsor, regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 Because you request general
guidance and your request does not involve a particular decision(s), we render informal
assistance (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71; regulation 18329.2)
QUESTIONS
1. Assuming Commissioner Fisher recuses herself from participation, drscussron
and voting on her client's project as a planning commissioner:
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear before the planning commission on
behalf of a client?
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client?
2. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher contact planning commission staff on
behalf of a client?
' Govern-"rrt Code sections 8 1 000 - 9 I 0 14. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 2
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client?
3. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before an
agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over which the
planning commission has budgetary control?
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control?
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact staff of such an agency on behalf of
a client?
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact staff of such an agency on
behalf of a client?
4. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before the
town council?
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the town
council on behalf of a client?
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact town council staff (which is
shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client?
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client?
5. Does regulation 18702.4(b)(a) apply to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity as
an urban designer?
6. For purposes of regulation 18702.4(bX4), what are "drawings or submissions
of an architectural, engineering or similar nature"?
7. May Commissioner Fisher submit on behalf of a client material to staff other
than technical drawings assuming that she has no other contact with staff with respect to
her client?
CONCLUSIONS
1. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client.


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 3
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client.
2. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client.
3. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not appear on behalf
of a client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or
over which the planning commission has budgetary control.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control.
(c) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact staff of
such an agency on behalf of a client.
(d) Commissioner Fisher's partner may contact staff of such an agency on
behalf of a client.
4. 
(a) Because the town council is not an agency appointed by or subject to
the budgetary control of the planning commission, the commissioner may appear
before the town council so long as she does not act or purport to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, the planning commission to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the town council.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear before the town
council on behalf of a client.
(c) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact town
council staff (which is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a
client.
(d) Commissioner Fisher's partner may contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client.
5. Yes. Regulation 18702.4(b)(4) applies to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity
as an urban designer.
6. Forpurposes of regulationl8702.4(b)(4), "drawings or submissions of an
architecfural, engineering or similar nature" is limited to technical drawings or
submissions to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before the agency.


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 4
7. Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not submit material to staff on
behalf of a client other than technical drawings, assuming that she has no other contact
with staff with respect to her client.
FACTS
Town of Windsor Planning Commissioner, Lois Fisher is a fifty-fifty partner in a
business entitled, "Fisher & Hall Urban Design." As urban designers, Ms. Fisher and her
firm lay out streets, buildings, schools, parks, etc. for public and private clients in a way
that creates walkable neighborhoods and towns as an alternative to sprawl. She advises
her clients on appropriate road widths to calm traffic, curb radii, and design options that
would help to create a unique sense of place. Her firm typically presents plans and codes
to design review, planning commissions and city and town councils.
As part of the work performed for clients, Ms. Fisher and her firm often submit
maps and drawings on behalf of their clients who have applications pending before
various govemmental agencies.
Ms. Fisher is a planning commissioner with the Town of Windsor. She anticipates
that in the future she and her firm may have clients that are seeking various entitlements
from the town. Ms. Fisher, desiring to be proactive and obtain guidance relative to
potential conflict of interest issues, has raised a number of questions as to what the extent
and scope of her involvement may be with respect to these clients as a result of her
position as a planning commissioner with the Town of Windsor.
For purposes of this inquiry you want to assume that the clients are "sources of
income" to Ms. Fisher and her urban design firm, and that her clients will have projects
which will come before the planning commission and possibly, the town council.
ANALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their financial interests.
(Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from
"making," "participating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to
"influence" a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700 (bX1) - (8).)
However, your question concerns only the issue of "participation," thus we limit our
discussion to step 2 ofthe standard analysis.
Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
Definitions: The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public
official "make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her]


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 5
official position to influence a goverrmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has
reason to know he [the official] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation
18700(bx2).)
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making,"
"participating in making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, and which also
provide certain exceptions. (Regulations I 8702- 1 87 02.4.)
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the offrcial, acting within
the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) You state in the facts that the
council members and the planning commissioners wish to vote on these
developments.
A public official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting
within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental deci sion. (Regulation 1 87 02.2.)
a
a
o
There are still two rules to consider as to whether a public official uses or attempts to
use his or her official position to "influence" a govemmental decision. The first rule
applies when the relevant govemmental decision is within or before the public
official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control
of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) Regulation18702.3(a) goes
on to state:
"...[T]he official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the
official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer."
Under this rule, attempts to influence include appearances or contacts on behalf of
a client.
A different rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is withrn or
before an agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation
18702.3(b).) Under this rule,
"...[T]he official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 6
or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an
agency."
Exceptions: The Commission has recognized an exception for presentation of
architectural, engineering, or similar drawings by the official to his or her own agency.
As a professional architect/urban planner, Commissioner Fisher would fall within this
exception. Pursuant to regulation I 87 02.4(b):
"(b) Notwithstanding Title2, California Code of Regulations,
section 18702.3(a), an official is not attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision of an agency covered by
that subsection if the official:
.I 
'T
il. . .It
"(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection with a
proceeding before any agency. However, this provision applies only if the
official has no other direct oral or written contact with the agency with
regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except for necessary
contact[3] with agency staff conceming the processing or evaluation of the
drawings or submissions prepared by the official."tal
Thus, this exception is limited to the preparation of technical documents
The basis for this exception was set forth in the staff memorandum submitted to
the Commission to explain the exception. The memorandum stated:
"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on the
professional activities of planning commissioners or city councilmembers
who are architects and engineers. There was general agreement that
planning commissionerfs]/architects should not actually represent clients
before the planning commission. It was also agreed that such architects
and engineers should be able to prepare the drawings and plans for clients,
even though the drawings and plans would have to be submitted to the
planning commission. There seemed to be general consensus among the
city attorneys that this exception is necessary in order to allow the local
architects and engineers to continue to practice their profession when they
act as planning commissioners or councilmembers." (Staff Memorandum
to the Commission, dated Apnl29,l985.)
3 'Necessary contact" has been narrowly construed to allow an official only to respond to
questions from agency staff regarding the evaluation of drawings and submissions prepared by the official
or relating to their movement through the approval process. (Thomson Advice Letter, No. I-00-239 and
Holbert Advice Letter, No. I-90-080.)
a Finally, the exception would not allow an official to contact agency staff with respect to any
drawing or submission prepared by someone other than the official. (Smilh Advice Letter, No. 4-96-022.)


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 7
Specific Questions:
1. Assuming Commissioner Fisher recuses herselffrom participation, discttssion
and voting on her client's project as a planning commissioner:
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear before the planning commission on
behalf of a client?
Commissioner Fisher may not contact, or appear before, or otherwise attempt to
influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the planning commission.
Attempts to influence include appearances or contacts on behalf of a client.
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from representing a client
before the commissioner's agency even when the partner uses letterhead that includes the
commissioner's name. (Novak Advice Letter, No. I-97-365.)
2. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher contact planntng commission staffon
behalf of a client?
Absent an exception, the commissioner is prohibited from influencing the
decision in question. As noted above, the commissioner is attempting to use "her official
position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
fficer, employee or consultant of the agency" on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. This includes planning commission staff.
2. 
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner contact planning
commisston staff on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from contacting planning
commission staff on behalf of a client, as discussed in question I (b) above.
3. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before an
agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over which the
planning commission has budgetary control?
The same rule set forth in Question 2(a) applies to appearances before an agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. Absent
an exception, the commissioner would be prohibited from appearing.


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 8
3. 
(b) Moy Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from representing a client
before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over which the
planning commission has budgetary control, as discussed in question 1(b) above.
3. 
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact staff of such an agency on behalf of
a client?
Absent an exception, the commissioner may not contact staff.
3. 
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact staffof such an agency
on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from contacting staff on
behalf of a client.
4. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before the
town cotmcil?
Because the town council is not an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the planning commission, the commissioner is only considered to be
"influencing" the decision if the commissioner, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, the planning
commission to any member, officer, employee or consultant of the town council.
Assuming she does not present herself as acting for or on behalf of the planning
commission, her appearance will not be prohibited.
4. 
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the town
council on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from appearing before the
town council on behalf of a client.
4. 
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact town council staff (which is shared
with the planning commission) on behalf of a client?
In the Farrell Advice Letter, No. I-03-l2l,we analyzed a similar question and
stated:
"I-Jnder the facts you provide, a city planner is assigned by the
Department to review a particular application and present various analyses
of the project before a number of city agencies, including the Board. This
sharing of staffing assignments means that a city planner is considered to


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 9
simultaneously represent each of these multiple agencies until a particular
agency's role in the review process has come to a conclusion. Because this
sharing includes a staff role with the Board, a public official is prohibited
from attempting to influence a shared staff member about a decision that
the Board eventually will consider. (Larmore Advice Letter, No. A-00-
275; Lyions Advice Letter, No. A-94-013; West Advice Letter, No. A-88-
413.)"
Your question poses a similar question with respect to shared staff between the
planning commission and town council. Consistent with Farrell, the commissioner may
meet or otherwise communicate with the shared town council/planning commissioner
staff, but only if a final decision is made by the planning commission on the matter with
no possibility of reconsideration before the planning commission.
4. 
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf ,f o client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from contacting staff on
behalf of a client.
5. Does regulation 18702.4(b)(4) apply to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity
as an urban designer?
While not expressly mentioned in the regulation, it appears that maps and
drawings prepared by an urban designer on behalf of clients falls within this exception.
The regulation, by its terms, is not limited solely to drawings submitted by architects and
engineers, but rather includes drawings and submissions of a "similar nature."
For example, in the Grocott Advice Letter, No. 4-02-028,the exception applied
to a public official who was also a professional building designer. In the Smith Advice
Letter, No. 4-96-022,the exception applied to a public official who was employed as
project manager and lead consultant to provide graphic design and production services to
the Port of San Francisco's Draft Waterfront Land Use Plan. In the Dorsey Advice
Letter, No. 4-94-284, the exception was applied to a public official who operated a
general construction business as a sole proprietorship. In O'Haire Advice Letter, No. A-
9l-566 the exception was applied to timber harvest plans prepared by a public official
who was also a professional forester.
In light of the prior interpretations of the exception, we conclude that it also
applies to Commissioner Fisher's designs and submissions of a technical nature
submitted as an urban designer.


File No. I-04-160
Page No. 10
6. For purposes of regulation 18702-4(b)(4), what are "drawings or submtssions
of an architectural, engineering or similar nature"?
The exception is limited to technical drawings or submissions to be used by a
client in connection with a proceeding before the agency. The preparation of non-
technical documents or other materials would not fall within this exception. (Levinger
Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.)
7. May Commissioner F'isher submit material other than technical drawings to
staffon behalf of a client?
Preparation of non-technical documents or other materials would not fall within
this exception and would still be prohibited. (Levinger Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel
By:
John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
Jwwjg

sections.conclusion:
1. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client.
2. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client.
3. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not appear on behalf
of a client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or
over which the planning commission has budgetary control.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control.
(c) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact staff of
such an agency on behalf of a client.
(d) Commissioner Fisher's partner may contact staff of such an agency on
behalf of a client.
4. 
(a) Because the town council is not an agency appointed by or subject to
the budgetary control of the planning commission, the commissioner may appear
before the town council so long as she does not act or purport to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, the planning commission to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the town council.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear before the town
council on behalf of a client.
(c) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact town
council staff (which is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a
client.
(d) Commissioner Fisher's partner may contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client.
5. Yes. Regulation 18702.4(b)(4) applies to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity
as an urban designer.
6. Forpurposes of regulationl8702.4(b)(4), "drawings or submissions of an
architecfural, engineering or similar nature" is limited to technical drawings or
submissions to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before the agency.
7. Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not submit material to staff on
behalf of a client other than technical drawings, assuming that she has no other contact
with staff with respect to her client.

sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their financial interests.
(Section 81001(b).) Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from
"making," "participating in making," or otherwise using his or her official position to
"influence" a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted a standard eight-step analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700 (bX1) - (8).)
However, your question concerns only the issue of "participation," thus we limit our
discussion to step 2 ofthe standard analysis.
Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.
Definitions: The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public
official "make[s], participate[s] in making or in any way attempt[s] to use his [or her]
official position to influence a goverrmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has
reason to know he [the official] has a financial interest." (Section 87100; regulation
18700(bx2).)
The Commission has adopted a series of regulations which define "making,"
"participating in making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, and which also
provide certain exceptions. (Regulations I 8702- 1 87 02.4.)
A public official "makes a govemmental decision" when the offrcial, acting within
the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits
his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual agreement on
behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.) You state in the facts that the
council members and the planning commissioners wish to vote on these
developments.
A public official "participates in making a govemmental decision" when, acting
within the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive or
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to the
decisionmaker regarding the governmental deci sion. (Regulation 1 87 02.2.)

o
There are still two rules to consider as to whether a public official uses or attempts to
use his or her official position to "influence" a govemmental decision. The first rule
applies when the relevant govemmental decision is within or before the public
official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control
of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) Regulation18702.3(a) goes
on to state:
"...[T]he official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any
member, officer, employee or consultant of the agency. Attempts to
influence include, but are not limited to, appearances or contacts by the
official on behalf of a business entity, client, or customer."
Under this rule, attempts to influence include appearances or contacts on behalf of
a client.
A different rule applies when the relevant governmental decision is withrn or
before an agency other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by
or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation
18702.3(b).) Under this rule,
"...[T]he official is attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his
or her agency to any member, officer, employee or consultant of an
agency."
Exceptions: The Commission has recognized an exception for presentation of
architectural, engineering, or similar drawings by the official to his or her own agency.
As a professional architect/urban planner, Commissioner Fisher would fall within this
exception. Pursuant to regulation I 87 02.4(b):
"(b) Notwithstanding Title2, California Code of Regulations,
section 18702.3(a), an official is not attempting to use his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision of an agency covered by
that subsection if the official:

il. . .It
"(4) Prepares drawings or submissions of an architectural,
engineering or similar nature to be used by a client in connection with a
proceeding before any agency. However, this provision applies only if the
official has no other direct oral or written contact with the agency with
regard to the client's proceeding before the agency except for necessary
contact[3] with agency staff conceming the processing or evaluation of the
drawings or submissions prepared by the official."tal
Thus, this exception is limited to the preparation of technical documents
The basis for this exception was set forth in the staff memorandum submitted to
the Commission to explain the exception. The memorandum stated:
"The cities were basically concerned about limitations on the
professional activities of planning commissioners or city councilmembers
who are architects and engineers. There was general agreement that
planning commissionerfs]/architects should not actually represent clients
before the planning commission. It was also agreed that such architects
and engineers should be able to prepare the drawings and plans for clients,

a Finally, the exception would not allow an official to contact agency staff with respect to any
drawing or submission prepared by someone other than the official. (Smilh Advice Letter, No. 4-96-022.)
Specific Questions:
1. Assuming Commissioner Fisher recuses herselffrom participation, discttssion
and voting on her client's project as a planning commissioner:
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear before the planning commission on
behalf of a client?
Commissioner Fisher may not contact, or appear before, or otherwise attempt to
influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the planning commission.
Attempts to influence include appearances or contacts on behalf of a client.
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from representing a client
before the commissioner's agency even when the partner uses letterhead that includes the
commissioner's name. (Novak Advice Letter, No. I-97-365.)
2. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher contact planntng commission staffon
behalf of a client?
Absent an exception, the commissioner is prohibited from influencing the
decision in question. As noted above, the commissioner is attempting to use "her official
position to influence the decision if, for the purpose of influencing the decision, the
official contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member,
fficer, employee or consultant of the agency" on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer. This includes planning commission staff.
2. 
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner contact planning
commisston staff on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from contacting planning
commission staff on behalf of a client, as discussed in question I (b) above.
3. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before an
agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over which the
planning commission has budgetary control?
The same rule set forth in Question 2(a) applies to appearances before an agency
appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public official's agency. Absent
an exception, the commissioner would be prohibited from appearing.
3. 
(b) Moy Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from representing a client
before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over which the
planning commission has budgetary control, as discussed in question 1(b) above.
3. 
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact staff of such an agency on behalf of
a client?
Absent an exception, the commissioner may not contact staff.
3. 
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact staffof such an agency
on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from contacting staff on
behalf of a client.
4. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before the
town cotmcil?
Because the town council is not an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the planning commission, the commissioner is only considered to be
"influencing" the decision if the commissioner, for the purpose of influencing the
decision, acts or purports to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, the planning
commission to any member, officer, employee or consultant of the town council.
Assuming she does not present herself as acting for or on behalf of the planning
commission, her appearance will not be prohibited.
4. 
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the town
council on behalf of a client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from appearing before the
town council on behalf of a client.
4. 
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact town council staff (which is shared
with the planning commission) on behalf of a client?
In the Farrell Advice Letter, No. I-03-l2l,we analyzed a similar question and
stated:
"I-Jnder the facts you provide, a city planner is assigned by the
Department to review a particular application and present various analyses
of the project before a number of city agencies, including the Board. This
sharing of staffing assignments means that a city planner is considered to
simultaneously represent each of these multiple agencies until a particular
agency's role in the review process has come to a conclusion. Because this
sharing includes a staff role with the Board, a public official is prohibited
from attempting to influence a shared staff member about a decision that
the Board eventually will consider. (Larmore Advice Letter, No. A-00-
275; Lyions Advice Letter, No. A-94-013; West Advice Letter, No. A-88-
413.)"
Your question poses a similar question with respect to shared staff between the
planning commission and town council. Consistent with Farrell, the commissioner may
meet or otherwise communicate with the shared town council/planning commissioner
staff, but only if a final decision is made by the planning commission on the matter with
no possibility of reconsideration before the planning commission.
4. 
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf ,f o client?
The Act would not prohibit the commissioner's partner from contacting staff on
behalf of a client.
5. Does regulation 18702.4(b)(4) apply to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity
as an urban designer?
While not expressly mentioned in the regulation, it appears that maps and
drawings prepared by an urban designer on behalf of clients falls within this exception.
The regulation, by its terms, is not limited solely to drawings submitted by architects and
engineers, but rather includes drawings and submissions of a "similar nature."
For example, in the Grocott Advice Letter, No. 4-02-028,the exception applied
to a public official who was also a professional building designer. In the Smith Advice
Letter, No. 4-96-022,the exception applied to a public official who was employed as
project manager and lead consultant to provide graphic design and production services to
the Port of San Francisco's Draft Waterfront Land Use Plan. In the Dorsey Advice
Letter, No. 4-94-284, the exception was applied to a public official who operated a
general construction business as a sole proprietorship. In O'Haire Advice Letter, No. A-
9l-566 the exception was applied to timber harvest plans prepared by a public official
who was also a professional forester.
In light of the prior interpretations of the exception, we conclude that it also
applies to Commissioner Fisher's designs and submissions of a technical nature
submitted as an urban designer.
6. For purposes of regulation 18702-4(b)(4), what are "drawings or submtssions
of an architectural, engineering or similar nature"?
The exception is limited to technical drawings or submissions to be used by a
client in connection with a proceeding before the agency. The preparation of non-
technical documents or other materials would not fall within this exception. (Levinger
Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.)
7. May Commissioner F'isher submit material other than technical drawings to
staffon behalf of a client?
Preparation of non-technical documents or other materials would not fall within
this exception and would still be prohibited. (Levinger Advice Letter, No. I-88-328.)

and voting on her client's project as a planning commissioner:
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear before the planning commission on
behalf of a client?
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client?
2. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher contact planning commission staff on
behalf of a client?

18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 14; regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client?
3. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before an
agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over which the
planning commission has budgetary control?
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control?
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact staff of such an agency on behalf of
a client?
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact staff of such an agency on
behalf of a client?
4. 
(a) May Commissioner Fisher appear on behalf of a client before the
town council?
(b) May Commissioner Fisher's business partner appear before the town
council on behalf of a client?
(c) May Commissioner Fisher contact town council staff (which is
shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client?
(d) May Commissioner Fisher's partner contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client?
5. Does regulation 18702.4(b)(a) apply to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity as
an urban designer?
6. For purposes of regulation 18702.4(bX4), what are "drawings or submissions
of an architectural, engineering or similar nature"?
7. May Commissioner Fisher submit on behalf of a client material to staff other
than technical drawings assuming that she has no other contact with staff with respect to
her client?

CONCLUSION: 1. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear before the
planning commission on behalf of a client.
2. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may contact planning
commission staff on behalf of a client.
3. 
(a) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not appear on behalf
of a client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or
over which the planning commission has budgetary control.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear on behalf of a
client before an agency which is appointed by the planning commission or over
which the planning commission has budgetary control.
(c) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact staff of
such an agency on behalf of a client.
(d) Commissioner Fisher's partner may contact staff of such an agency on
behalf of a client.
4. 
(a) Because the town council is not an agency appointed by or subject to
the budgetary control of the planning commission, the commissioner may appear
before the town council so long as she does not act or purport to act on behalf of,
or as the representative of, the planning commission to any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the town council.
(b) Commissioner Fisher's business partner may appear before the town
council on behalf of a client.
(c) Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not contact town
council staff (which is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a
client.
(d) Commissioner Fisher's partner may contact town council staff (which
is shared with the planning commission) on behalf of a client.
5. Yes. Regulation 18702.4(b)(4) applies to Commissioner Fisher in her capacity
as an urban designer.
6. Forpurposes of regulationl8702.4(b)(4), "drawings or submissions of an
architecfural, engineering or similar nature" is limited to technical drawings or
submissions to be used by a client in connection with a proceeding before the agency.
7. Absent an exception, Commissioner Fisher may not submit material to staff on
behalf of a client other than technical drawings, assuming that she has no other contact
with staff with respect to her client.
